title: Check Voting Eligibility
short: Confirm you meet the basic rules to vote in your area.
whyItMatters: If you are not eligible yet, you can still plan what to do next (like registering when you turn 18 or updating citizenship records).
whatToDo: Check the minimum voting age in your country or region
Confirm citizenship and residency requirements
If you recently moved, check if you must register in your new address
commonMistakes: Assuming turning 18 automatically registers you
Using outdated rules from a different region
Waiting until the last week to confirm eligibility
tips: If rules vary by region, use the Region selector in this app
If you are unsure, verify using the official election authority website when available

title: Register as a Voter
short: Submit your voter registration (or application) before the deadline.
whyItMatters: Registration is usually the step that makes you officially eligible to cast a ballot on polling day.
whatToDo: Find the registration method: online, form submission, or in-person
Prepare identity and address documents
Submit the application and save your reference number
commonMistakes: Missing the deadline
Submitting incomplete documents
Not saving the application reference
tips: Take a clear photo/scan of documents to avoid rejection
Double-check spelling of name and address before submitting

title: Verify Your Name in the Voter List
short: Check that your name appears correctly in the official voter list/electoral roll.
whyItMatters: If your name is missing or incorrect, you may not be allowed to vote, or your voting location may be wrong.
whatToDo: Search the voter list using your details or reference number
Confirm spelling, address, and assigned polling station
Request correction if anything is wrong
commonMistakes: Checking too early and never re-checking later
Ignoring small errors (name spelling, address)
Not submitting a correction request during the allowed window
tips: Set a reminder to re-check closer to polling day
Keep screenshots/receipts for correction requests

title: Understand Election Announcement & Important Dates
short: Learn key dates like nomination, polling, and counting.
whyItMatters: Election steps are time-bound. Knowing dates helps you avoid missing deadlines and prepares you for polling day.
whatToDo: Note the registration and correction deadlines
Save polling day and expected counting/results dates
Track your region’s announcements through official sources
commonMistakes: Relying on social media for dates
Assuming all regions have the same schedule
Not planning travel/time-off for polling day
tips: Add key dates to your calendar
If dates change, prioritize official notices

title: Candidate Nomination & Campaign Phase
short: This is when candidates file nominations and campaigns happen.
whyItMatters: Understanding this phase helps you recognize official information, rules, and typical timelines without getting overwhelmed.
whatToDo: Know the nomination window and when final candidate lists are published
Learn basic campaign rules (quiet periods, conduct codes, etc.) where applicable
Use official voter education resources for neutral information
commonMistakes: Confusing campaign messages with official voting instructions
Missing the final candidate list release date
Sharing unverified election claims
tips: For voting procedure questions, prefer election authority guidance
Focus on logistics: registration, dates, location, documents

title: Locate Your Polling Station
short: Find where you must go to vote and plan your trip.
whyItMatters: Most systems require you to vote at your assigned station. Knowing it early reduces polling-day stress.
whatToDo: Confirm your assigned polling station from the voter list
Check the address and nearest landmark
Plan your travel time and accessibility needs
commonMistakes: Assuming you can vote at any station
Arriving late because you didn’t plan travel time
Not checking station changes/updates
tips: Do a quick route check a day before if possible
If you need assistance, plan with a friend or family member

title: Prepare Required Documents
short: Keep the right ID and documents ready before polling day.
whyItMatters: Bringing approved identification helps you complete verification quickly and reduces the chance of being turned away.
whatToDo: Check the list of accepted IDs for your region
Carry originals if required
Keep documents safe and easy to access on the day
commonMistakes: Bringing an unaccepted ID
Carrying a damaged/expired ID if validity matters
Forgetting to bring anything at all
tips: Pack your polling-day essentials the night before
If you do not have the main ID, check if alternates are allowed in your region

title: Vote on Polling Day
short: Follow the official process at your polling station and cast your vote.
whyItMatters: Knowing what happens inside the polling station helps first-time voters feel confident and avoid mistakes.
whatToDo: Arrive during polling hours
Follow staff instructions and verification steps
Cast your vote carefully and confirm completion
commonMistakes: Arriving after closing time
Carrying prohibited items (if rules apply)
Rushing and making an avoidable marking/selection mistake
tips: If you have a question, ask polling staff before you finalize your choice
Stay calm—polling stations are designed to guide you step-by-step

title: Vote Counting Process
short: Votes are counted following official procedures to ensure accuracy.
whyItMatters: Understanding counting builds trust in the process and helps you ignore rumors and misinformation.
whatToDo: Follow counting updates through official channels
Understand that counting can take time
Be cautious about unverified early claims
commonMistakes: Assuming results must be immediate
Sharing unofficial counts as final
Misunderstanding what partial results mean
tips: Look for official result dashboards or announcements
Expect updates in stages, especially in large elections

title: Results Declaration
short: Final results are officially declared after verification.
whyItMatters: Official declaration indicates the results are finalized according to the rules and verification checks.
whatToDo: Check final results from official sources
Understand that final certification may happen after preliminary counts
Save links/screenshots of official announcements for reference
commonMistakes: Treating unofficial sources as final
Confusing preliminary updates with official declaration
Falling for fake result screenshots
tips: Prefer official election authority websites or verified announcements
If in doubt, wait for the official declaration
